sections.heading: Definition and Origin
sections.content: Cutaneous melanoma is a malignant neoplasm arising from melanocytes within the skin. Melanocytes are pigment-producing cells derived from the neural crest (ectoderm) during embryonic development.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation and Progression
sections.content: Cutaneous melanoma most commonly presents as an pigmented skin lesion with irregular borders, color variation, and progressive growth, though amelanotic variants exist. The disease originates in the epidermis (melanoma in situ, stage 0) and may invade through the basement membrane into the dermis (invasive melanoma), subsequently metastasizing via lymphatic and hematogenous routes to regional lymph nodes (stage III) and distant organs (stage IV) [28]. Melanoma is distinguished from other cutaneous malignancies by its propensity for early lymphatic dissemination and its immunogenicity, which underlies the efficacy of modern immune checkpoint inhibitor therapies [592-594].

sections.heading: Staging System
sections.content: The current AJCC 8th Edition staging system incorporates tumor thickness (Breslow depth), ulceration status, mitotic rate, sentinel lymph node (SLN) status, number of involved nodes, presence of in-transit/satellite/microsatellite metastases, and serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) for distant disease [28,85,86].

sections.heading: Noncutaneous Melanomas
sections.content: Noncutaneous melanomas arising from mucosal surfaces, the uveal tract, or leptomeninges are considered biologically distinct diseases with different staging, treatment, and prognostic implications and are addressed in separate NCCN Guidelines [36-41].

sections.heading: Clinical Subtypes
sections.content: Clinical subtypes of cutaneous melanoma include low cumulative sun damage (low-CSD) melanomas—typically superficial spreading or nodular subtypes; high cumulative sun damage (high-CSD) melanomas—typically lentigo maligna subtype characterized histologically by marked solar elastosis and lentigo maligna growth pattern; and acral melanomas arising on the soles, palms, or subungual sites [MS-2]. Pure desmoplastic melanoma, defined as ≥90% of invasive melanoma associated with prominent stromal fibrosis, is a histologic variant with distinct biological behavior, including lower sentinel lymph node positivity rates [footnote l, ME-1A].
